retrievedChunks.title: 1440p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7800 XT 1440p value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7800 XT is a strong value target for 1440p gaming with 16GB VRAM. It favors raster performance per dollar over CUDA ecosystem support.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: Intel Arc B580 value profile
retrievedChunks.content: Intel Arc B580 Limited Edition provides 12GB VRAM, 190W total board power, AV1 media support, and a $249 recommended customer price. It is relevant to value 1080p/1440p builds, while application and game-specific driver behavior should be verified for specialized workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

retrievedChunks.title: Radeon RX 9070 XT RDNA 4 profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Radeon RX 9070 XT has 16GB GDDR6, a 304W typical board power rating, and a 750W minimum PSU recommendation from AMD. It is a strong raster and FSR-oriented 4K gaming option, but users requiring CUDA-first software should still prefer NVIDIA.

retrievedChunks.title: Case clearance constraints
retrievedChunks.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7900 XTX raster value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7900 XTX offers 24GB VRAM and excellent high-resolution raster performance. It is compelling for 4K gaming when CUDA compatibility is not required.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

## Summary
This $3,992 build delivers a high-performance AI workstation with an NVIDIA GPU (CUDA required), 128GB system RAM, and sufficient VRAM for 70B Q4 inference and FLUX image generation. The system is optimized for quiet operation and meets all required constraints within budget.

## Trade-offs
The power headroom fails [compatibility], meaning the PSU may be insufficient under sustained full load, risking stability during extended AI workloads. The selected GPU’s VRAM capacity directly impacts model fitting without CPU offload [K1], and higher-VRAM options like the RTX 4090 (24GB) [K2] or RTX 5090 would better suit the 70B Q4 requirement but exceed budget or availability.

## Upgrade path
Upgrading the PSU to a higher-wattage unit would resolve the power headroom failure and ensure stable operation. A future GPU upgrade to a model with 32GB+ VRAM (e.g., RTX 5090) would better accommodate larger AI models and improve inference speed [K1][K2].

retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: Power supply headroom rule
retrievedChunks.content: A power supply should exceed estimated sustained system draw by at least 35 percent. Additional headroom can reduce fan noise and preserve room for future GPU upgrades.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 5 7600 value profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 5 7600 preserves AM5 upgradeability while leaving more of a constrained gaming budget for the GPU.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

retrievedChunks.title: X670E workstation expansion
retrievedChunks.content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

retrievedChunks.title: 64GB DDR5 workload fit
retrievedChunks.content: A 64GB DDR5 kit is the default workstation capacity for local AI, development, and video editing when the budget allows.

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: ATX 3.1 and modern GPU power planning
retrievedChunks.content: For high-draw current GPUs, an ATX 3.1 supply with a native 12V-2x6 cable simplifies cabling and is designed around modern transient requirements. Wattage still must satisfy deterministic sustained-load headroom; the connector standard does not make an undersized PSU acceptable.

retrievedChunks.title: Memory capacity for development
retrievedChunks.content: Docker, local databases, IDEs, browsers, and emulators compete for memory. 32GB is a practical baseline; 64GB provides meaningful headroom for heavier container and mobile build workflows.

## Summary
This $4,872 build pairs a Ryzen 9 9950X3D with an RTX 5090, 128GB RAM, and a 4TB NVMe SSD, fully compatible and well-suited for Unreal Engine 5 shader compilation, Blender Cycles, and large C++ builds at 4K/60fps.

## Trade-offs
The RTX 5090’s 32GB VRAM is overkill for current 4K gaming but essential for large local AI models, while the 9950X3D’s large cache benefits compilation workloads but adds cost versus a non-3D Ryzen 9 [K2][K4].

## Upgrade path
The AM5 platform supports future CPU upgrades, and the high-wattage PSU allows adding a second GPU or more storage without replacing the power supply.
